texto: His youth was a wingless Dodo.

texto: There is nothing more exasperating than reading in contemporary guidebooks disparagements of places that are deemed to be "seedy." Do the writers not notice that such places are invariably crowded with people? When a neighborhood is described as "seedy" by some Lonely Planet prude, I immediately head there.
explicacao: Pergunta retórica de Lawrence Osborne que provoca passagem do tempo e prioridades. Ao questionar “There is nothing more exasperating than reading in contemporary guidebooks disparagements of plac…”, o texto convida o leitor a revisar crenças sobre reflexao, travel, there, com foco em viagem e reflexao.

frase_original: There is nothing more exasperating than reading in contemporary guidebooks disparagements of places that are deemed to be "seedy." Do the writers not notice that such places are invariably crowded with people? When a neighborhood is described as "seedy" by some Lonely Planet prude, I immediately head there.

texto: For a long time I had wanted to take leave of Planet Tourism, to find one of those places that occasionally turn up in the middle pages of newspapers in far-flung cities, in which--we are told--a mad loner has been discovered who has lost all contact with the modern world. It seems inevitable that this desire will one day be listed in the Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of the American Psychiatric Association as Robinson Crusoe Syndrome.
